one_line_takeaway: ROMAN pairs a multi-head-attention Transformer scenario generator with an LLM-scored traffic-law severity/occurrence weighting scheme to out-cover and out-diversify prior ADS law-violation testing tools (ABLE, LawBreaker) on Baidu Apollo in CARLA, at the cost of substantially longer training time.
key_finding: ROMAN covered all 81 traffic-law clauses across every road structure, whereas ABLE missed 1/3/3 clauses (S1/S2/S3) and LawBreaker missed 5/9/8/4 clauses (S1-S4). On S3 (bidirectional intersection), ROMAN's mean violation count exceeded ABLE's by 7.91% and LawBreaker's by 55.96%; its max-per-scenario violation count was 18 vs. ABLE's 16 and LawBreaker's 9. For the 'High Rate>8' metric on S3, ROMAN reached 24% vs. ABLE's 19% and LawBreaker's 14%; for 'High Rate>10', ROMAN reached 9% vs. ABLE's 5% and LawBreaker's 0%. Scenario diversity (DTW distance) averaged across S1-S4: ROMAN's median 74.05 vs. ABLE's 62.19 (+19.07%) and LawBreaker's 39.62 (+86.90%). Ablations showed the LLM weighting mechanism improves coverage of high-severity clauses (vs. ROMAN-unweighting), and 8 attention heads outperformed 4 and 2 heads on DTW diversity (74.05 vs 69.92 vs 65.64 median). The proxy reward model matched simulator-computed reward closely (Mean Relative Error 7.35%; mean reward 0.2616 vs. simulator's 0.2671) while cutting per-scenario evaluation cost from 86.8409s to 0.0095s (~9000x speedup). Cross-LLM weighting consistency (Table VI) showed pairwise Spearman correlations >0.90 across GPT/Gemini/Claude/Qwen and MAE below 0.07 on the 4.0-point scale, with per-clause token costs of 5984 (GPT), 6213 (Gemini), 5418 (Claude), 5166 (Qwen). Training/generation temperature sweep (Table V) found temperature 100/1.00 as the best validity-diversity tradeoff (Completeness 0.91, Satisfaction 0.90, Distinct-1/2/3 0.25/0.48/0.61, Self-BLEU 0.25).
core_contribution: ROMAN (Reward-Orchestrated Multi-head Attention Network) is a scenario-generation approach for testing Automated Driving Systems (ADS) against traffic law violations. It combines (1) a multi-head attention (Transformer) network that models complex multi-vehicle, multi-signal interactions to generate scenario action sequences, and (2) an LLM-based traffic-law weighting mechanism that scores each violation clause on severity and occurrence (0.0-4.0 scale, w = S*O/4.0) to bias reward-driven generation toward high-risk, safety-critical violations. Generation is trained with a GFlowNet-style flow-matching loss (Eq. 5) using a lightweight proxy reward model in place of costly simulator evaluation, and is non-iterative at inference time. The system was implemented and evaluated by testing Baidu Apollo v8.0 inside the CARLA simulator, ported from the LGSVL-based baselines ABLE and LawBreaker.
threat_model: Not applicable — this is a software testing / quality-assurance paper for autonomous driving systems, not a security paper. There is no adversary, attacker capability, or attack surface being modeled. The 'risk' modeled is regulatory/safety risk (traffic law violations such as running red lights or unsafe overtaking) discovered via systematic test-scenario generation, evaluated with Signal Temporal Logic (STL) robustness semantics via the RTAMT monitor, not an adversarial or security threat model.
limitations: Authors' stated threats to validity: (1) ROMAN's training time is substantially longer than ABLE/LawBreaker (e.g., 14,813s vs 10,224s on S3) due to the attention mechanism's computational cost, though inference time (44-53s) is comparable to ABLE (33-41s); authors suggest multi-GPU parallelism as mitigation. (2) The LLM-based severity/occurrence weighting depends on the completeness/quality of the underlying legal knowledge base and may inherit LLM training-data biases; mitigated only partially via expert review. (3) ROMAN cannot guarantee coverage of extremely rare edge cases due to search-space constraints and stochastic traffic dynamics. Additional limitations evident from the text but not flagged by authors as 'threats': evaluation is limited to a single ADS (Baidu Apollo v8.0) in a single simulator (CARLA) and a subset of 81 Chinese traffic-law clauses selected specifically because they were STL-formalizable, so generalization to other legal systems, ADS stacks, or non-STL-expressible laws is untested; LawBreaker has no training phase so training-time comparisons for it are not meaningful (marked '–' in Table IV).

datasets_used: 81 STL-formalized clauses derived from Chinese traffic regulations
China's Administrative Measures for Road Traffic Safety Violation Scoring (knowledge base source)
China's Motor Vehicle Driver Training Syllabus (knowledge base source)
Evaluation scenario sets: 1024 scenarios each for road structures S1 and S2, 512 each for S3 and S4
quantitative_results: S3 mean violation count: ROMAN 6.41 vs ABLE 5.94 (+7.91%) vs LawBreaker 4.11 (+55.96%)
S3 max violations per scenario: ROMAN 18, ABLE 16, LawBreaker 9
S3 High Rate>8: ROMAN 24%, ABLE 19%, LawBreaker 14%
S3 High Rate>10: ROMAN 9%, ABLE 5%, LawBreaker 0%
Clause coverage misses: ABLE missed 1/3/3 clauses (S1/S2/S3, 0 stated for S4); LawBreaker missed 5/9/8/4 clauses (S1/S2/S3/S4); ROMAN missed 0
Average median DTW distance across S1-S4: ROMAN 74.05, ABLE 62.19 (+19.07%), LawBreaker 39.62 (+86.90%)
Training time on S3: ROMAN 14,813s, ABLE 10,224s, LawBreaker n/a (no training phase)
Inference time on S3: ROMAN 52s, ABLE 38s, LawBreaker 62s
Proxy model vs simulator: MRE 7.35%, proxy reward 0.2616 vs simulator reward 0.2671, proxy eval time 0.0095s vs simulator 86.8409s
Cross-LLM weight consistency: pairwise Spearman correlations 0.90-0.954, MAE 0.031-0.068 on a 4.0 scale
Best temperature setting (train 100 / gen 1.00): Completeness 0.91, Satisfaction 0.90, Distinct-1 0.25, Distinct-2 0.48, Distinct-3 0.61, Self-BLEU 0.25, Entropy 7.04, Coverage 0.88
baselines_compared: ABLE (GFlowNet-based ADS law-violation scenario generator, ported from LGSVL to CARLA for this study)
LawBreaker (genetic-algorithm fuzzing-based ADS law-violation testing tool, ported from LGSVL to CARLA for this study)
ROMAN-unweighting (ablation: ROMAN without the LLM traffic-law weighting mechanism)
ROMAN with 4 and 2 attention heads (ablation of the 8-head default)
